EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low dropped southward across New Mexico and brought heavy snow to the southern Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 6 to 8 inches of snow was measured between Cloudcroft and Sunspot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved eastward across the desert southwest and induced a deep surface low in the Texas panhandle. A tight surface gradient and the mixing down of strong winds aloft resulted in high winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was measured 15 miles south of Hachita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved eastward across the desert southwest and induced a deep surface low in the Texas panhandle. A tight surface gradient and the mixing down of strong winds aloft resulted in high winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 67 mph were measured at White Sands Missle Range Headquarters. A gust of 97 mph was measured near San Augustine Pass. Windows were blown out of vehicles at the WSMR main post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved eastward across the desert southwest and induced a deep surface low in the Texas panhandle. A tight surface gradient and the mixing down of strong winds aloft resulted in high winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 71 mph was measured just southwest of High Rolls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued low level moisture and good shear in the low to mid levels of the atmosphere produced isolated severe thunderstorms in Mora and Colfax counties.
Continued low level moisture and good shear in the low to mid levels of the atmosphere produced isolated severe thunderstorms in Mora and Colfax counties.

An unstable atmosphere with plenty of low level moisture and decent shear triggered isolated severe weather over Roosevelt county.

A slow moving upper level trough crossed New Mexico on the 5th, bringing isolated reports of high winds across central and eastern portions of the state.
A sharp dry line helped focus a few thunderstorms over southeast New Mexico during the late afternoon hours. One severe storm over extreme eastern Chaves county produced penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dry line over eastern New Mexico on the 10th helped focus the development of isolated severe thunderstorms over De Baca and Roosevelt counties. Hail around one inch in diameter was reported.
A sharp dry line over eastern New Mexico on the 10th helped focus the development of isolated severe thunderstorms over De Baca and Roosevelt counties. Hail around one inch in diameter was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was at the Melrose Bombing Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dry line over eastern New Mexico helped focus thunderstorm development on the evening of the 10th. One thunderstorm near Melrose turned severe and produced strong wind gusts at the Melrose Bombing Range.

The dry line over eastern New Mexico become active on the afternoon and evening of the 11th as scattered thunderstorms formed. A few of these storms turned severe, producing large hail, mainly over the south central mountains and northeast plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail along highway 456 and 406.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry line over eastern New Mexico become active on the afternoon and evening of the 11th as scattered thunderstorms formed. A few of these storms turned severe, producing large hail, mainly over the south central mountains and northeast plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail at the Lincoln State Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry line over eastern New Mexico become active on the afternoon and evening of the 11th as scattered thunderstorms formed. A few of these storms turned severe, producing large hail, mainly over the south central mountains and northeast plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail near Thomas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry line over eastern New Mexico become active on the afternoon and evening of the 11th as scattered thunderstorms formed. A few of these storms turned severe, producing large hail, mainly over the south central mountains and northeast plains.
The storm that had been parked along the California coast finally moved inland on the 18th and created isolated high winds over the Central High Plains.
A slow moving upper level trough crossed New Mexico on the 5th, bringing isolated reports of high winds across central and eastern portions of the state.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 58mph was also recorded at 1046am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active dry line over the eastern plains of New Mexico again helped focus the development of thunderstorms, with isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong size hail at the Cuervo Exxon station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active dry line over the eastern plains of New Mexico again helped focus the development of thunderstorms, with isolated reports of large hail.

A weak upper level disturbance combined with a fresh supply of low level moisture to trigger a couple of severe thunderstorms over the central mountain chain.

A storm off the California coast on the 4th and 5th finally moved inland on the 6th and reached the four corners area early on the 7th. This allowed for plenty of moisture to surge into New Mexico ahead of the storm. A long duration snow event occurred over the southern San Juan and Sangre De Cristo mountains with around two feet of snow. Six to 12 inches of snow was common over the western and central mountains, while 3 to 6 inches fell in the western valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 2 feet of snow fell above 7500 feet with 2 to 10 inches below 7500 feet. There were areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that deepened off the northern California coast on January 22nd and 23rd weakened as it moved inland on the 24th and crossed new Mexico on the 25th as a weak trough of low pressure. However, the storm was able to draw enough moisture into the state to produce light to moderate snowfall over primarily the northwest third of New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of up to 4 inches around Farmington and Shiprock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shower produced gusty winds at the Raton Aiport.
A cold front drove southwest across northeast New Mexico and combined with an upper level low over southeast New Mexico to produce severe thunderstorms across east central parts of the state.

Slow moving thunderstorms in weak northeast flow aloft near high pressure over the Four Corners area resulted in heavy rain and isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm dropped heavy rain over the west slopes of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains resulting in excessive runoff and flash flooding.  Water flowed across State Road 592 southeast of Chupadero.  A rural road was washed out on Tesuque Pueblo and several yards were flooded there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing Colorado on February 8th tightened the mid level pressure gradient, which mixed down to the surface, resulting in isolated high winds in Los Alamos and near Ruidoso.

An upper level disturbance crossing Colorado on February 8th tightened the mid level pressure gradient, which mixed down to the surface, resulting in isolated high winds in Los Alamos and near Ruidoso.

A storm along the Pacific northwest coast moved south and strengthened into a closed low over northern Baja California on the 14th. The storm moved east on the 15th and crossed extreme southern New Mexico on the 16th. The result was a band of significant snowfall, primarily across east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general area of 3 to 7 inches of snow fell over the northern portions of the Central High Plains, including southeast Santa Fe county and southwest San Miguel county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm along the Pacific northwest coast moved south and strengthened into a closed low over northern Baja California on the 14th. The storm moved east on the 15th and crossed extreme southern New Mexico on the 16th. The result was a band of significant snowfall, primarily across east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 6 inches of snow fell over Guadalupe county early on the 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported in far northwest De Baca county on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of storms crossed New Mexico on the 25th, bringing high winds to several locations from late on the 24th through the morning of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS also reported a sustained wind speed of 43 mph at 1053pm on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of storms crossed New Mexico on the 25th, bringing high winds to several locations from late on the 24th through the morning of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cannon AFB ASOS also reported a sustained wind speed of 40 mph at 855am.

A storm that deepened off the northern California coast on January 22nd and 23rd weakened as it moved inland on the 24th and crossed new Mexico on the 25th as a weak trough of low pressure. However, the storm was able to draw enough moisture into the state to produce light to moderate snowfall over primarily the northwest third of New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of up to 4 inches fell below 7500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm along the west coast helped increase winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere on the 27th, leading to an isolated strong wind gust at the Sierra Blanca Airport near Ruidoso.

Strong northeasterly winds funneled through the Guadalupe Pass behind a strong cold front.  The winds were the result of a strong thermal/pressure gradient and persisted for nearly 12 hours.  Other locations near the Guadalupe Mountains, such as the Batdraw RAWS site, experienced very brief high wind criteria observations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Batdraw RAWS site recorded sustained winds of 40 to 41 mph for two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough embedded in a broad cyclonic flow over New Mexico swept across northeast New Mexico. While snow was isolated, it was quite impressive, with snow amounts between 10 and 15 inches around Capulin, Des Moines and Folsom, while 4 inches fell around Grenville.

A short wave trough embedded in a broad cyclonic flow over New Mexico swept across northeast New Mexico. While snow was isolated, it was quite impressive, with snow amounts between 10 and 15 inches around Capulin, Des Moines and Folsom, while 4 inches fell around Grenville.
A strong trough of low pressure aloft increased the surface gradient over northeast New Mexico and led to isolated and brief high wind gusts.

A strong storm off the California coast moved inland to Arizona then weakened as it cross New Mexico. A good moisture surge ahead of the storm helped produce moderate snows over the Northwest Mountains.

An upper level trough over the western U.S. moved across the Rockies into the Plains, resulting in strong winds mixing down to the surface behind a Pacific cold front.  The high winds lasted for more than 12 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained measured winds of 40 mph or more, with gusts peaking at 70 mph, occured at KGDP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low approached southeast New Mexico and west Texas during the late hours of November 10th.  Mid-level winds associated with the upper level low, just ahead and near the base, increased to speeds near 50 knots.  Momentum transfer of these winds resulted in high winds for the Guadalupe Mountains of Eddy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two hours of sustained wind speeds above 40 mph were observed at the KGDP ASOS, with the highest being 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeasterly winds funneled through the Guadalupe Pass behind a strong cold front.  The winds were the result of a strong thermal/pressure gradient and persisted for nearly 12 hours.  Other locations near the Guadalupe Mountains, such as the Batdraw RAWS site, experienced very brief high wind criteria observations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KGDP ASOS site recorded sustained wind speeds of 40 mph or greater for 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass remained over eastern New Mexico on the 29th with plenty of low level moisture and shear to generate another round of severe thunderstorms. Large hail was first reported over De Baca and Harding counties between 200 and 300 pm MDT, then was reported over the far eastern plains counties along the Texas border between 300 pm and 700 pm MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated four inches deep.

A storm dropping south into the Great Basin strengthened the southwest flow aloft and at the surface over New Mexico on May 21st. This resulted in strong to high winds over most of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts between 60 and 62 mph and sustained wind speeds of 40 to 44 mph were reported at the Sierra Blanca Airport between 210pm and 530pm.

A deep upper low dropped southeastward from the Pacific Northwest, then slowly tracked eastward along the U.S./Mexico border. As the system approached southern New Mexico, a back door cold front brought in low level cold air from the plains. Heavy snow was not nearly as widespread as expected, but 4 inches did fall in the Silver City area (6000 feet ASL).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell in Silver City, with 5 to 6 inches in the higher elevations to the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong upper level jet moving across the region and a deepening surface low pressure system over Oklahoma resulted in high winds across the plains of southeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reached in Carlsbad around noon. The event lasted for approximately five hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of intense low pressure moved along the New Mexico and Colorado line into Kansas.  West winds with speeds of 50 to 80 knots at 500 mb mixed down to the surface creating sustained winds of between 45 and 55 mph and gusts over 60 mph for several hours across much of central and eastern New Mexico.

A weak steering flow within a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in a slow moving thunderstorms in southern New Mexico which dumped very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter reported 3 inches of rain within an hour in Chaparral, and the surrounding neighborhood was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak steering flow within a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in a slow moving thunderstorms in southern New Mexico which dumped very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upslope conditions within a tropical air mass resulted in 6 to 10 inches of rainfall within 6 hours. The area is remote, so the only effects were a ranch under water (according to the rancher) and a couple of highways flooded. Dark Ridge Observatory near Weed reported more than 6 inches of rain from the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak steering flow within a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in a slow moving thunderstorms in southern New Mexico which dumped very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 9 was flooded east of Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak steering flow within a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in a slow moving thunderstorms in southern New Mexico which dumped very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of thunderstorms moved over an area already saturated from heavy rain the previous day. Arroyos overflowed and low water crossings were flooded. Highways that were closed included Highway 9 between Columbus and Hachita, Highway 26 between Deming and Hatch, and Highway 27 south of Hillsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper impulse within a northeast flow aloft triggered severe thunderstorms over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was measured at the Las Cruces Airport during a severe thunderstorm. However, the winds were estimated at 65 mph near Mayfield High School were several trees were blown over and minor structural damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper impulse within a northeast flow aloft triggered severe thunderstorms over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter relayed a report from a rancher of trees blown down on his ranch.

A potent upper level low moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico while a cold front backed southwest across the eastern half of the state. Four to eight inches of snow were common across the east central and northeast plains with 8 to 12 inches along parts of the interstate 40 in eastern New Mexico, along the east slopes of the central mountains chain and in the upper Rio Grande Valley and the southeast slopes of the Jemez Mountains.

Back-tracking meant an extra 40 miles of driving, but the extra gas was worth the spared life.

Strong to severe thunderstorms first developed over the southwest portion of the state and spread quickly north and east into the central valley and eastern plains by mid afternoon. High winds also developed by the afternoon over the far western portions of the state.

A weak upper level disturbance crossed New Mexico on January 9th, creating a few strong sustained winds and wind gusts over east central and northeast portions of the state.

A storm off the California coast on the 4th and 5th finally moved inland on the 6th and reached the four corners area early on the 7th. This allowed for plenty of moisture to surge into New Mexico ahead of the storm. A long duration snow event occurred over the southern San Juan and Sangre De Cristo mountains with around two feet of snow. Six to 12 inches of snow was common over the western and central mountains, while 3 to 6 inches fell in the western valleys.

February started off windy and white as a storm over the Pacific Northwest  dove southeast on the 3rd and crossed New Mexico on the 4th. A weaker disturbance brought in moisture on the 2nd and produced light snowfall over the northwest mountains. But the bulk of the snow and wind came on the 3rd through the night of the 5th. The higher snowfall totals were over the northwest mountains, while high winds blasted portions of the central mountain chain onto the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 1 to 2 feet were common in the high country.

A storm off the northern California coast on the 22nd crossed New Mexico on the 23rd, bringing moderate to heavy snow to the northern and western mountains, Northwest Plateau and Upper Rio Grande Valley. Snow amounts were generally 4 to 8 inches over the lower elevations and 8 to 16 inches over the higher terrain. Tierra Amarilla recorded 20 inches of snow. Meanwhile, high winds again blasted parts of central and eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 mph was also reported at 555pm MST. High winds (both sustained and peak gusts) were recorded at the Sierra Blanca Airport from around 600 am to 600 pm MST on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm off the northern California coast on the 25th and 26th moved inland on the 27th, crossing New Mexico early on the 28th. Meanwhile, a second storm from the Pacific Northwest raced southeast across the state on the night of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th. The first storm pulled sub-tropical moisture north into the state and tightened the pressure gradient aloft. The second storm had less moisture to work with, but produced a few bursts of heavy snow and strong winds across northern and central New Mexico. Heavy rain in the upper Gila region of the Southwest mountains resulting in isolated flooding and flash flooding events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Los Alamos also reported a peak wind gusts of 61 mph at 650am on January 29th. The general public reported a measured peak wind gust of 60 mph in Rio Rancho at 1208am on January 29th. High winds caused roof damage in Los Alamos. A portion of a roof was blown off an old barrack style apartment building. Two pine trees fell and damaged another roof at 47th Street and Urban Avenue, while a large pine tree behind Fuller Lodge fell and damaged a rose garden. Another tree snapped and fell onto a truck's camper shell on Torreon Street in Quemazon, causing damage. Many homes in Los Alamos lost shingles and there were a number of power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm off the northern California coast on the 25th and 26th moved inland on the 27th, crossing New Mexico early on the 28th. Meanwhile, a second storm from the Pacific Northwest raced southeast across the state on the night of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th. The first storm pulled sub-tropical moisture north into the state and tightened the pressure gradient aloft. The second storm had less moisture to work with, but produced a few bursts of heavy snow and strong winds across northern and central New Mexico. Heavy rain in the upper Gila region of the Southwest mountains resulting in isolated flooding and flash flooding events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 2 inches across the lower elevations of Sandoval county to 14 inches near the Colorado border.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to 11 inches of snow fell over the southern portion of the Northeast Highlands, including central San Miguel county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February started off windy and white as a storm over the Pacific Northwest  dove southeast on the 3rd and crossed New Mexico on the 4th. A weaker disturbance brought in moisture on the 2nd and produced light snowfall over the northwest mountains. But the bulk of the snow and wind came on the 3rd through the night of the 5th. The higher snowfall totals were over the northwest mountains, while high winds blasted portions of the central mountain chain onto the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts reached 58 mph or higher between 1130 am on the 3rd through 1010 am on the 4th with gusts between 58 and 71 mph. Sustained winds of 40 mph or higher occurred between 1030 am on the 3rd through 1250 pm on the 4th with speeds between 40 and 54 mph. A tree fell on a house in Ruidoso and power lines were down across roads near the Ruidoso Downs racetrack. There were numerous reports of power surges and outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level disturbance over the Central Rockies barreled south into New Mexico late Monday night dropping moderate to heavy snow across a large portion of the state, along with gusty winds over much of the east central and southeast plains. Wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph were reported ahead of this system across portions of the eastern plains. The heaviest snow fell Monday night into Tuesday where upwards of 6-10 inches of snow was reported over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 mph was also reported at 12:49 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough ejected east from a storm off the California coast on the 12th and crossed New Mexico on the 13th. A tight mid level gradient pressed toward the surface and produced high winds over portions of the central mountain vhain and eastern high plains from the 13th through the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 66 mph was also reported at 530 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, strong upper level trough approached New Mexico from the west. In response, a deep surface trough developed in the southern plains to the northeast of the area. The tight surface pressure gradient and mixing down of strong winds aloft resulted in high winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 65 mph was measured just southwest of High Rolls in the southern Sacramento Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, strong upper level trough approached New Mexico from the west. In response, a deep surface trough developed in the southern plains to the northeast of the area. The tight surface pressure gradient and mixing down of strong winds aloft resulted in high winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 86 mph was measured near Aguirre Springs State Park on the eastern slopes of the Organ Mountains, resulting in a few shingles blown off a roof. Minor building damage occurred in Las Cruces according to a local TV station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough spread abundant subtropical moisture into much of southern New Mexico. Since snow levels were high (above 8000 feet), the result was up to two inches of rain on an existing snowpack. The excessive runoff due to heavy rain and snowmelt led to the flooding of streams feeding into the Gila River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (2 inches) on a snowpack led to a flood wave which badly damaged highway bridges south of Gila Hot Springs. Highway 15 was impassable for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds of 40 to 50 knots at 700 mb and 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb translated to the surface during the late morning and afternoon hours.  The result was sustained west winds around 40 mph and gusts over 60 mph from the southwest mountains and lower Rio Grande valley eastward across the central and south central mountains as well as the east central and southeast plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew down a large sign at a travel center.  The sign struck a parked vehicle.

A potent upper level low moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico while a cold front backed southwest across the eastern half of the state. Four to eight inches of snow were common across the east central and northeast plains with 8 to 12 inches along parts of the interstate 40 in eastern New Mexico, along the east slopes of the central mountains chain and in the upper Rio Grande Valley and the southeast slopes of the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped heavy snow over the east mountains and the east central plains along interstate 40.  Snow reports generally totaled 4 to 6 inches from the Estancia Valley eastward across Guadalupe into eastern San Miguel and western Quay counties.  Heavier bands of snow occurred over the east mountains and parts of eastern Guadalupe County.  Edgewood reported 10 inches while 8 inches fell near Sandia Park and 12 inches piled up at Newkirk.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed in Carlsbad early Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low over Arizona created strong southwest flow over New Mexico which resulted in high winds across the central high plains, the east mountains and the upper Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were recorded by the Clines Corners ASOS and by spotters in the east mountains, central highlands and upper Rio Grande Valley near Stanley and Placitas.  Shingles were blown off the roof of a house near McIntosh.  Tree limbs 3 inches in diameter were broken and a bolted down vent cover on a greenhouse roof was blown off near Espanola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low over Arizona created strong southwest flow over New Mexico which resulted in high winds across the central high plains, the east mountains and the upper Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were recorded by the Clines Corners ASOS and by spotters in the east mountains, central highlands and upper Rio Grande Valley near Stanley and Placitas.  Shingles were blown off the roof of a house near McIntosh.  Tree limbs 3 inches in diameter were broken and a bolted down vent cover on a greenhouse roof was blown off near Espanola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds of 40 to 50 knots at 700 mb and 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb translated to the surface during the late morning and afternoon hours.  The result was sustained west winds around 40 mph and gusts over 60 mph from the southwest mountains and lower Rio Grande valley eastward across the central and south central mountains as well as the east central and southeast plains.

Strong mid level winds of 40 to 50 knots at 700 mb and 60 to 80 knots at 500 mb translated to the surface during the late morning and afternoon hours.  The result was sustained west winds around 40 mph and gusts over 60 mph from the southwest mountains and lower Rio Grande valley eastward across the central and south central mountains as well as the east central and southeast plains.

A potent upper level low moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico while a cold front backed southwest across the eastern half of the state. Four to eight inches of snow were common across the east central and northeast plains with 8 to 12 inches along parts of the interstate 40 in eastern New Mexico, along the east slopes of the central mountains chain and in the upper Rio Grande Valley and the southeast slopes of the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped heavy snow over the upper Rio Grande Valley and the east slopes of the Jemez Mountains.  Snow reports include 11 inches at Los Alamos, 12 inches at White Rock and Espanola and 15 inches at Nambe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico while a cold front backed southwest across the eastern half of the state. Four to eight inches of snow were common across the east central and northeast plains with 8 to 12 inches along parts of the interstate 40 in eastern New Mexico, along the east slopes of the central mountains chain and in the upper Rio Grande Valley and the southeast slopes of the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped heavy snow over the Sangre De Cristo Mountains and the northeast highlands. Snow reports include 6 to 7 inches at Santa Fe, Eagle Nest, Capulin, Cimarron and Springer, 9 inches near Angel Fire and Manuelitas, and 10 inches at Cowles and Mineral Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of intense low pressure moved along the New Mexico and Colorado line into Kansas.  West winds with speeds of 50 to 80 knots at 500 mb mixed down to the surface creating sustained winds of between 45 and 55 mph and gusts over 60 mph for several hours across much of central and eastern New Mexico.

Strong winds in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, in advance of a series of upper level disturbances located over Arizona and along the West Coast, resulted in widespread high winds over portions of southeast New Mexico and the mountains of southwest Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed in Carlsbad during the early afternoon on Monday, January 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough passed to the north of New Mexico.  Southwest winds around 50 knots at 500 mb mixed to the surface and produced wind gusts around 60 mph near Las Vegas and Ruidoso.

A series of upper level shortwave troughs moved across the region, affecting portions of southeast New Mexico during the early afternoon and evening hours.  Southwest to westerly winds, associated with the mid-level jet max in the base of the trough, increased across much of southeast New Mexico, the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains and portions of the northern Permian Basin.

Strong westerly winds developed across portions of the Davis and Guadalupe mountains and Southeast New Mexico Plains ahead of a broad upper level trough axis.  The mid to upper jet max, mixing and surface pressure gradient resulted in 60 to 70 mph winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple structures were damaged in and around the Artesia, NM area during the late morning and early afternoon hours.  At 1230MST, the Gary Fanning Farms (located 7SE of Artesia) had 8 side roll irrigation sprinkler systems blown away and destroyed.  Meanwhile, a hay barn in Atoka, NM was blown down and a storage shed was destroyed.||In addition, localized visibilities of less than 1 mile due to blowing dust resulted in a 4 vehicle accident on HWY 285 and Haldeman Road.  A trailer was also blown onto the same highway around 1115MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds developed across portions of the Davis and Guadalupe mountains and Southeast New Mexico Plains ahead of a broad upper level trough axis.  The mid to upper jet max, mixing and surface pressure gradient resulted in 60 to 70 mph winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof and multiple telephone poles were blown down near Love Street in Lovington, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level shortwave troughs moved across the region, affecting portions of southeast New Mexico during the early afternoon and evening hours.  Southwest to westerly winds, associated with the mid-level jet max in the base of the trough, increased across much of southeast New Mexico, the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains and portions of the northern Permian Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 300 pound porta-potties were blown across an industrial yard near Calsbad, NM.  Winds were estimated to be 70 mph.  The same spotter reported billboard signs were also blown down along US HWY 285.||Three people were injured in a two car accident on US HWY 285 between the cities of Loving and Carlsbad, NM.  Blowing dust in the area reduced visibilities to approximately 100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level shortwave troughs moved across the region, affecting portions of southeast New Mexico during the early afternoon and evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power/phone poles were snapped on US 62/180 just south of Hobbs, New Mexico.  In addition, a roof collapsed at a Hobbs restaurant and another business had their roof blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico while a strong cold front drove south across the state.  Three to five inches of snow fell from the northeast plains to the central highlands with 6 to 12 inches over the higher elevations of the central mountain chain.  Snow amounts were less across the east central and southeast plains, generally from 1 to 3 inches.  However, wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph accompanied the snow, creating extensive blowing snow, reduced visibilities and dangerous travel conditions.

A weak upper level disturbance combined with a fresh supply of low level moisture to trigger a couple of severe thunderstorms over the central mountain chain.
An upper level disturbance combined with a dry line to trigger thunderstorms across eastern New Mexico, some of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down briefly north of Gladstone.

The first outbreak of severe weather occurred across eastern New Mexico on the afternoon and evening of the 5th. A storm system churning off the northern Baja California coast was able to draw significant low level moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the state on the 5th, helping trigger the outbreak. Isolated storms became severe shortly after noon on the 5th and continued past 800 pm in the evening.

The Trigo Wildfire, which began on April 15th, continued during the first part of May before becoming contained on May 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trigo Wildfire, which began on April 15th, grew by an additional two thousand acres during the first 2 days of May. While substantial property damage occurred in April, little additional property damage was reported in May as the fire was mainly confined to wilderness areas. The fire became contained on May 11th after consuming a total of 13,709 acres.

A human caused fire turned into a large wildfire during several days of strong winds.  Very dry conditions were present prior to the wildfire due to a lack of precipitation in the preceding weeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trigo Wildfire began on the west slopes of the Manzano Mountains and was initially spread by southwest wind gusts to 35 mph. The fire reached Osha Peak during the evening of April 16th. On the 20th, the fire spread rapidly northeast due to 40 mph winds. It entered flatter terrain on the east side of the Manzanos, and by April 21st, 3750 acres were burned including nine homes, nine outbuildings and two recreational vehicles. The 4800 acre fire was 95 percent contained by April 29th, but was fanned by strong southwest winds of 40 to 50 mph on the 30th, forcing the evacuation of Sufi and Apple Mountain Campgrounds and the Sherwood Forest subdivision, west of Torreon.  Over 50 additional homes and one communications tower were damaged or destroyed, mainly in the Sherwood Forest area as the fire grew to more than 11,000 acres.  The fire continued to be uncontained into the month of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed over southeast New Mexico during the afternoon of the 6th and continued into the early morning hours of the 7th. A slow moving storm over southwest New Mexico was responsible for the spotty severe weather.

An upper level trough was moving slowly over the Great Basin on the 4th. Mid level winds tightened and mixed down to the surface on the afternoon of the 4th, creating a few reports of high winds over the Capitan, northern Sacramento and Guadalupe mountains of Chaves county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was at the Dunken RAWS site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speed of 43mph was recorded for 20 minutes from 150-210pm MST.

A warm unstable airmass and a strongly sheared environment resulted in supercell thunderstorms across parts of New Mexico.  Severe storms began near the central mountain chain during the afternoon and spread across the plains during the evening, tracking eastward just to the north of Interstate 40.  Radar indicated several tornadoes along this track, but only one confirmed report was received, just north of Tucumcari.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong west winds behind the dryline and moist southeast surface flow resulted in strong storms throughout the area. Initial thunderstorm development occurred along this boundary, which was located from Carlsbad, southward through Fort Stockton. Additional upper level atmospheric support was provided by a passing upper level disturbance. The strength of thunderstorms was increased by having a moderately unstable and high wind shear environment.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball sized hail was reported on New Mexico State highway 529.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong west winds behind the dryline and moist southeast surface flow resulted in strong storms throughout the area. Initial thunderstorm development occurred along this boundary, which was located from Carlsbad, southward through Fort Stockton. Additional upper level atmospheric support was provided by a passing upper level disturbance. The strength of thunderstorms was increased by having a moderately unstable and high wind shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to golfball hail fell across the city of Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm unstable airmass and a strongly sheared environment resulted in supercell thunderstorms across parts of New Mexico.  Severe storms began near the central mountain chain during the afternoon and spread across the plains during the evening, tracking eastward just to the north of Interstate 40.  Radar indicated several tornadoes along this track, but only one confirmed report was received, just north of Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding of draws and roads east of Stanley, including Flat Lakes Road.

High based showers produced strong wind gusts at Cannon Air Force Base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cannon Air Force Base ASOS measured 59 mph while the runway wind sensor registered 71 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was moving slowly over the Great Basin on the 4th. Mid level winds tightened and mixed down to the surface on the afternoon of the 4th, creating a few reports of high winds over the Capitan, northern Sacramento and Guadalupe mountains of Chaves county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 70 foot Ponderosa pine tree was snapped in two by strong winds during the late afternoon of the 4th, one mile east of Ruidoso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm unstable airmass and a strongly sheared environment resulted in supercell thunderstorms across parts of New Mexico.  Severe storms began near the central mountain chain during the afternoon and spread across the plains during the evening, tracking eastward just to the north of Interstate 40.  Radar indicated several tornadoes along this track, but only one confirmed report was received, just north of Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 104 was washed out just south of the Quay-San Miguel County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of low level moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over central New Mexico to produce large hail and flash flooding. A thunderstorm produced a wet microburst and significant wind damage in Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of low level moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over central New Mexico to produce large hail and flash flooding. A thunderstorm produced a wet microburst and significant wind damage in Chaves County.

Plenty of low level moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over central New Mexico to produce large hail and flash flooding. A thunderstorm produced a wet microburst and significant wind damage in Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 80 mph struck the southeast side of Hagerman.  Outbuildings sustained heavy damage to doors and roofs and empty grain bins were destroyed.  In addition, several small to medium size trees were uprooted. An estimated 2 inches of rain in 30 minutes accompanied the wet microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm unstable airmass and a strongly sheared environment resulted in supercell thunderstorms across parts of New Mexico.  Severe storms began near the central mountain chain during the afternoon and spread across the plains during the evening, tracking eastward just to the north of Interstate 40.  Radar indicated several tornadoes along this track, but only one confirmed report was received, just north of Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell spawned at least one tornado on its eastward trek across San Miguel County.  A confirmed touchdown was reported and photographed north of Anton Chico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 23rd, lightning started a wildfire in heavy timber on the east side of the Manzano Mountains, not far from the area of the Trigo Fire, which had burned earlier in the spring.  Over 5000 acres were consumed before the fire was contained June 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Springs fire consumed 5478 acres on the east slopes of the Manzano Mountains about 3 to 6 miles west northwest of Tajique.  Six homes and ten outbuildings were destroyed in the fire in the Apache Canyon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system progressed eastward across the Baja of California, an upper level disturbance traversed over the region and provided needed atmospheric lift. Status clouds encompassing the area during the morning hours quickly dissipated, allowing for good surface heating. This combination of clearing and atmospheric lift along with abundant low level moisture set the stage for thunderstorm development across southeastern New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported at Brantley Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of low level moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over central New Mexico to produce large hail and flash flooding. A thunderstorm produced a wet microburst and significant wind damage in Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail lasted for 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms along the west slopes of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding near Chimayo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arroyo overflowed in Chimayo, sending floodwaters into one residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air aloft associated with a large upper low over the Great Basin provided sufficient instability to create severe thunderstorms across central New Mexico.
Cold air aloft associated with a large upper low over the Great Basin provided sufficient instability to create severe thunderstorms across central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed a carport constructed of corrugated sheet metal secured to a wooden frame. Part of the carport landed on a pickup truck, causing damage to it. The damage occurred in northwest Albuquerque near the intersection of Pequeno and 2nd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unusually strong late spring storm continued to spin over the Great Basin on the 23rd of May, resulting in more strong winds for portions of New Mexico.
The combination of plenty of moisture and slow movement of thunderstorms created torrential rains and several reports of flash flooding over western and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Rio Bravo and Broadway was washed out, while mudslides occurred on University Drive at Spirit Drive. Floodwaters deposited hail to a depth of 1 foot at the intersection of Clark Carr and Spirit Drive. A home was flooded on Arthur Road SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of plenty of moisture and slow movement of thunderstorms created torrential rains and several reports of flash flooding over western and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State road 601 was closed due to flooding from 2 to 5 miles northwest of U.S. 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of plenty of moisture and slow movement of thunderstorms created torrential rains and several reports of flash flooding over western and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water was observed over state road 173. The road was partially washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of plenty of moisture and slow movement of thunderstorms created torrential rains and several reports of flash flooding over western and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on State Road 53 near Grants, State Road 605 north of Grants and State Road 279 near Seboyeta and Cebolletita.  Street flooding in Grants reached a depth of one foot.  Floodwaters also topped a bridge near San Rafael.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell split over the Holloman AFB area, with the left moving cell tracking northeastward into Tularosa and the right moving cell eastward into Alamogordo. Hail up to tennis ball size (but mostly half dollar size) accumulated to a depth of nearly 3 inches in Tularosa accompanied by a wet microburst with wind gusts estimated at 80 mph. The other cell produced golfball to lime size hail in Alamogordo, though with much less intensity than the Tularosa cell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size was measured by Holloman AFB weather observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell split over the Holloman AFB area, with the left moving cell tracking northeastward into Tularosa and the right moving cell eastward into Alamogordo. Hail up to tennis ball size (but mostly half dollar size) accumulated to a depth of nearly 3 inches in Tularosa accompanied by a wet microburst with wind gusts estimated at 80 mph. The other cell produced golfball to lime size hail in Alamogordo, though with much less intensity than the Tularosa cell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from quarter to golf ball size, with occasional hail stones the size of tennis balls accumulated to about 3 inches in the Tularosa area. The hail was driven by wind gusts up to 80 mph, and broke windows, damaged roofs and sidings to more than 500 homes in Tularosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell split over the Holloman AFB area, with the left moving cell tracking northeastward into Tularosa and the right moving cell eastward into Alamogordo. Hail up to tennis ball size (but mostly half dollar size) accumulated to a depth of nearly 3 inches in Tularosa accompanied by a wet microburst with wind gusts estimated at 80 mph. The other cell produced golfball to lime size hail in Alamogordo, though with much less intensity than the Tularosa cell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst estimated at 80 mph destroyed a double wide mobile home, flipped several others, unroofed a few homes, and blew down several trees, power poles and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human caused grass fire spread quickly in the area southwest of the town of Hagerman.  The fire consumed 1000 acres before it was contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A human caused grass fire just southwest of Hagerman was spread by strong southwest winds and consumed 1000 acres. The wildfire destroyed a vacant mobile home, a camper trailer and 2 sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of plenty of moisture and slow movement of thunderstorms created torrential rains and several reports of flash flooding over western and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of Belen and the surrounding communities experienced some degree of flooding with water 2 to 3 feet deep in some locations. Flash flooding caused a mudslide on the Interstate 25 exit ramp at Camino Del Llano. A nearby diner was also flooded. Flood waters also entered a few homes. In addition, the wall of a welding shop succombed to the pressure of flood waters on it and buckled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once again, low level moisture with decent low to mid level shear and a weak upper level disturbance led to isolated severe thunderstorms over the far eastern plains.
Once again, low level moisture with decent low to mid level shear and a weak upper level disturbance led to isolated severe thunderstorms over the far eastern plains.
Once again, low level moisture with decent low to mid level shear and a weak upper level disturbance led to isolated severe thunderstorms over the far eastern plains.
Once again, low level moisture with decent low to mid level shear and a weak upper level disturbance led to isolated severe thunderstorms over the far eastern plains.
A deep tap of subtropical moisture combined with a weak disturbance aloft to produce heavy rain over Gallup, resulting in flash flooding in the downtown area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused water up to three feet deep to flow through downtown Gallup, on either side of Interstate 40. A rock slide occurred near the intersection of West Jefferson Avenue and Highway 491, causing a large boulder to crush three transients who were seeking shelter from the rain. A section of Highway 491 was also flooded 9 miles north of Gallup. A number of homes were flooded in Gallup, including some as far south as Nizhona Boulevard. Sundance Road was also flooded east of Gallup, in a subdivision between Rehoboth and Church Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over Colorado provided adequate wind shear and instability to produce a severe weather outbreak across central and eastern sections of New Mexico.  Several of the storms became tornadic while others produced large hail, damaging winds and flooding rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down west of Sumner Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture, possibly aided by the remnants of Edouard, fueled thunderstorms with heavy rain over Albuquerque. Several reports of flash flooding were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flash flooding was observed across much of central and north|central Albuquerque on the evening of the 8th. Carlisle road was especially hit hard. Two feet of water was flowing over Carlisle and Montgomery and some apartments had water in them near this intersection. High water was also reported at Carlisle and Lomas, Candelaria and Edith, and Osuna and Jefferson. One person was rescued from the Interstate 40 diversion channel which had several feet of fast moving water in it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once again, low level moisture with decent low to mid level shear and a weak upper level disturbance led to isolated severe thunderstorms over the far eastern plains.
A deep tap of subtropical moisture over the state helped produce thunderstorms with torrential downpours. One such storm brought flash flooding to the town of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Rio Bonito dislodged a couple of foot bridges at the Lincoln State Monument and moved them downstream approximately one eighth of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, moist atmosphere, aided by outflow boundaries, helped produce a few reports of funnel clouds, a brief tornado and heavy rain around Tucumcari. Heavy rain over the Sacramento Mountains also produced flash flooding along the Rio Penasco River in southwest Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police officer observed a tornado touching the ground for about two minutes near the Tucumcari airport, moving northeast. The Quay County Sun newspaper reported the tornado between the I-40 frontage road (on the south side) and county road 62, and east of county road AK. No damage was reported from this brief tornado as it crossed open fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, moist atmosphere, aided by outflow boundaries, helped produce a few reports of funnel clouds, a brief tornado and heavy rain around Tucumcari. Heavy rain over the Sacramento Mountains also produced flash flooding along the Rio Penasco River in southwest Chaves County.

A deep, moist atmosphere, aided by outflow boundaries, helped produce a few reports of funnel clouds, a brief tornado and heavy rain around Tucumcari. Heavy rain over the Sacramento Mountains also produced flash flooding along the Rio Penasco River in southwest Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the headwaters of the Rio Penasco and Agua Chituita Creek led to flooding of at least two ranches along the Rio Penasco river, south of U.S. route 82 in southwest Chaves county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest upper level flow was in place over west Texas and southeast New Mexico in advance of a broad upper level trough located over the western United states. Meanwhile, an upper level ridge of high pressure was centered across Louisiana. The primary focusing mechanism for thunderstorm initiation over the area was the dryline. The atmosphere became increasingly unstable east of this boundary as temperatures warmed and lapse rates steepened. The combination of instability along with a shallow layer of dry air near the surface set the stage for a primarily damaging wind threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured the wind gust and no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts as they moved southeast across east central sections of New Mexico.
Rich moisture and slow storm movement provided a good environment for  thunderstorms to produce flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 60 was flooded at mile marker 31 and State Road 601 was flooded between mile markers 2 and 4.  One house was flooded in Quemado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich moisture and slow storm movement provided a good environment for  thunderstorms to produce flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 434 was closed between mile markers 6 and 13 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich moisture and slow storm movement provided a good environment for  thunderstorms to produce flooding rains.
Rich moisture and slow storm movement provided a good environment for  thunderstorms to produce flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused major street flooding for the second consecutive evening in northeast Santa Fe.  Lorenzo Road had mud deposited nearly 2 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture brought heavy rain to the Santa Fe area, resulting in flash flooding on the northeast side of town as arroyos became torrents and streets flooded.  Locations affected include Morales Bridge, Circle Drive, Lorenzo Road and Cerro Gordo Road. One fatality occurred in this flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding struck the northeast part of Santa Fe. Arroyos became torrents and overflowed their banks in some instances. Major street flooding ensued in some locations, especially along Lorenzo Road which saw mud deposited two feet deep. One man drowned when he was swept down an arroyo. An unoccupied automobile was damaged when it was also swept down an arroyo. Flash flooding occurred at the Morales Bridge on the north side of town, and a home was flooded on Circle Dr nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture across New Mexico continued to fuel the development of thunderstorms capable of producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding.|Isolated flash flooding was observed in McKinley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 53 was closed in both directions between mile markers 35 and 36. Water was up to one foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture across New Mexico continued to fuel the development of thunderstorms capable of producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding.|Isolated flash flooding was observed in McKinley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. highway 491 was closed at mile marker 16 due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture across New Mexico continued to fuel the development of thunderstorms capable of producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding.|Isolated flash flooding was observed in McKinley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along highway 491 at the junction of Navajo route 34 near Little Water. Vehicles were unable to pass through the area between 1000 pm and 1200 am. One vehicle was stuck and had to be pulled out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts as they moved southeast across east central sections of New Mexico.
A southerly flow of moist, unstable air poured into New Mexico, causing |a few reports of flash flooding and large hail around the towns of Albuquerque and Bernalillo, and in the Jemez mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris pouring onto U.S. route 550 at mile marker 48 near the Dragon Fly area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front along with an upper impulse within northerly flow aloft triggered severe thunderstorms with large hail on June 20th. On the 21st, the front was still present but under increasing heights. The resulting warming trend increased dewpoint depressions and raised the microburst threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 65 mph knocked out power, toppled two large trees and closed the interstate (due to low visibility from blowing dust) in and around Deming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity was mainly confined to the northern Permian Basin and extreme southeastern New Mexico. A slowly moving cold front oriented east to west along this region provided sufficient atmospheric lift for the development of thunderstorms. Several funnel clouds were reported across the region. As supercells emerged, one storm produced a short lived tornado near Interstate 20 and was photographed by a storm chaser. Most storms in the afternoon travelled eastward at a brisk pace and produced hail along with gusty winds.

As an upper level low pressure system progressed eastward across the Baja of California, an upper level disturbance traversed over the region and provided needed atmospheric lift. Status clouds encompassing the area during the morning hours quickly dissipated, allowing for good surface heating. This combination of clearing and atmospheric lift along with abundant low level moisture set the stage for thunderstorm development across southeastern New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty police officer reported softball sized hail at the Washington Ranch. Windows were broken and the north side of a home was damaged as this storm moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dissipating thunderstorms over the Texas Panhandle produced severe outflow winds that moved into portions of southeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm induced outflow boundary produced high winds in Caprock for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent south to southeast winds continued to bring deep layer moisture to the area. Strong upslope surface flow in the higher terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico provided the needed atmospheric lift for thunderstorm development, even though instability and wind shear were only mild at best.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observation station measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 65 mph. A Texas Tech Mesonet station also measured a severe thunderstorm gust of 58 mph. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well established southerly flow kept moisture at higher levels throughout the region. A dryline located from Roswell through the Guadalupe Mountains slowly moved eastward during the afternoon and provided a focusing mechanism for storm development. The strength of thunderstorms was increased by the presence of a moderately unstable and higher wind shear environment.

Persistent south to southeast winds continued to bring deep layer moisture to the area. Strong upslope surface flow in the higher terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico provided the needed atmospheric lift for thunderstorm development, even though instability and wind shear were only mild at best.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 foot long gooseneck cattle trailer and center pivot irrigation system were blown over by strong thunderstorm winds. Additionally, five powerline poles were downed along New Mexico State Highway 206.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent south to southeast winds continued to bring deep layer moisture to the area. Strong upslope surface flow in the higher terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico provided the needed atmospheric lift for thunderstorm development, even though instability and wind shear were only mild at best.

Well established southerly flow kept moisture at higher levels throughout the region. A dryline located from Roswell through the Guadalupe Mountains slowly moved eastward during the afternoon and provided a focusing mechanism for storm development. The strength of thunderstorms was increased by the presence of a moderately unstable and higher wind shear environment.

A back door cold front and upper level trough combined to generate heavy rain over portions of northern and eastern New Mexico, with reports of flash flooding in Cimarron and Dora and south of Los Alamos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 206 had several spots with two to three feet of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front along with an upper impulse within northerly flow aloft triggered severe thunderstorms with large hail on June 20th. On the 21st, the front was still present but under increasing heights. The resulting warming trend increased dewpoint depressions and raised the microburst threat.

A back door cold front and upper level trough combined to generate heavy rain over portions of northern and eastern New Mexico, with reports of flash flooding in Cimarron and Dora and south of Los Alamos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 4 to 6 inches fell in and near the town of Cimarron.  Flash flooding occurred with several houses along U.S. Highway 64 flooded.  Water flowed one to two feet deep through town.  Two vehicles were washed off State Road 21.  One person was injured attempting to rescue one of the motorists who was trapped in their vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture associated with the remnants of tropical storm Lowell resulted in tropical funnel development which touched down briefly near Vaughn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tropical funnel briefly touched down near Vaughn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and a few embedded thunderstorms repeatedly produced rainfall over much of Eddy County. Although rainfall was not particularly heavy, persistent, moderate rains led to flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported two feet of water running across the intersection of County Road 206 and U.S. Highway 82.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak steering flow within a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in a slow moving thunderstorms in southern New Mexico which dumped very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two thunderstorms with very heavy rain passed over Truth or Consequences and Elephant Butte. Highways 51 and 195 were closed, and many other streets were impassable. Water entered businesses along  Broadway Street downtown. A 7 foot deep wall of water in Ash Canyon (east of Truth or Consequences) washed out Highway 51 and triggered mudslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and a few embedded thunderstorms repeatedly produced rainfall over much of Eddy County. Although rainfall was not particularly heavy, persistent, moderate rains led to flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in three feet of water flowing across County Road 401 southwest of Lakewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Former hurricane Dolly brought tropical moisture into New Mexico, dumping up to seven inches of rain around Ruidoso. This caused the Rio Ruidoso and Rio Bonito to rise well above flood stage and resulted in widespread and serious flooding around Ruidoso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread, serious flooding occurred from the upper canyon of the Rio Ruidoso downstream through the towns of Ruidoso, Hollywood and Ruidoso Downs as far east as Fox Caves. Flooding also occurred along the Rio Bonito from the dam at Bonito Lake downstream to State Road 48.  Flooding was extensive in Ruidoso, not only along the Rio Ruidoso, Cedar Creek and other small streams, but also throughout the town from the 6 to 7 inches of rainfall that fell there. One man was killed when we was swept away by the raging Rio Ruidoso, but no other substantial injuries were reported. A total of 500 structures, including campers and mobile homes were damaged. Around 200 houses were damaged, and some were completely destroyed. At least 13 bridges were washed out and several cars were washed downstream. Several dozen recreational vehicles and campers were damaged at an RV park at Ruidoso Downs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance over southern New Mexico combined with just enough moisture at low levels and some mid level drying to trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm over northwest Chaves County.

A weak upper level disturbance over southern New Mexico combined with just enough moisture at low levels and some mid level drying to trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm over northwest Chaves County.

An unstable atmosphere combined with plenty of moisture over eastern New Mexico to trigger strong winds and large hail over Quay, Roosevelt and Chaves Counties. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Roswell during the late evening and early morning hours.

An unstable atmosphere combined with plenty of moisture over eastern New Mexico to trigger strong winds and large hail over Quay, Roosevelt and Chaves Counties. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Roswell during the late evening and early morning hours.

An unstable atmosphere combined with plenty of moisture over eastern New Mexico to trigger strong winds and large hail over Quay, Roosevelt and Chaves Counties. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Roswell during the late evening and early morning hours.

An unstable atmosphere combined with plenty of moisture over eastern New Mexico to trigger strong winds and large hail over Quay, Roosevelt and Chaves Counties. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Roswell during the late evening and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inch diameter tree limbs were downed in the town of House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere combined with plenty of moisture over eastern New Mexico to trigger strong winds and large hail over Quay, Roosevelt and Chaves Counties. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Roswell during the late evening and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of law enforcement vehicles flooded on the north side of Roswell. One vehicle was washed away and later found on Berrendo road near Berrendo Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere combined with plenty of moisture over eastern New Mexico to trigger strong winds and large hail over Quay, Roosevelt and Chaves Counties. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Roswell during the late evening and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms snapped seven power poles in northeast Roswell. About 2500 residents of Roswell were without power for several hours. A trampoline was blown into a cinder block wall, damaging the wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Dolly moved northwestward into far west Texas, moving directly over west El Paso, then curved north and northeastward into Otero County New Mexico. Moisture from this system spread over most of southern New Mexico and lingered another 36 hours. Storm totals from early the 26th into the 27th peaked at more than 4 inches in the Santa Teresa Country Club area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were at least 5 road closures throughout the city due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple low water crossings were flooded. Other areas were only accessible to four wheel drive vehicles due to excessive debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increased potential for heavy rainfall existed across much of the region. The prevailing upper level pattern over the area led to weak storm steering currents.  This coupled with abundant atmospheric moisture resulted in thunderstorms, which in turn produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported six to eight inches of water flowing across several street intersections in Artesia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and a few embedded thunderstorms repeatedly produced rainfall over much of Eddy County. Although rainfall was not particularly heavy, persistent, moderate rains led to flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water flowed across the intersection of Roosevelt street and New Mexico State Highway 285/180.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and a few embedded thunderstorms repeatedly produced rainfall over much of Eddy County. Although rainfall was not particularly heavy, persistent, moderate rains led to flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported one foot of water flowing across New Mexico State Highway 401.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Dolly moved northwestward into far west Texas, moving directly over west El Paso, then curved north and northeastward into Otero County New Mexico. Moisture from this system spread over most of southern New Mexico and lingered another 36 hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture from the remnants of Dolly led to heavy rainfall and flash flooding in the town of Dona Ana. Several roads were barricaded and water entered numerous homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Dolly moved northwestward into far west Texas, moving directly over west El Paso, then curved north and northeastward into Otero County New Mexico. Moisture from this system spread over most of southern New Mexico and lingered another 36 hours. Storm totals from early the 26th into the 27th peaked at more than 4 inches in the Santa Teresa Country Club area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Dolly brought 2.5 to 3.5 inches of rain to southern Dona Ana County. Water was knee deep in Chaparral, and Highway 28 was flooded south of La Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries within a very moist air mass caused the development of thunderstorms with very heavy rain. Unfortunately, the ground was already saturated from rainfall on several preceding days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 3 inches of rain fell within an hour over already-saturated ground in the western portion of Deming. Major street flooding occurred, with most roads closed west of Highway 11. This same area was under 2 to 3 feet of water which entered homes and businesses. Power was out in the majority of the city as water logged power poles were easily knocked over by 45 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries within a very moist air mass caused the development of thunderstorms with very heavy rain. Unfortunately, the ground was already saturated from rainfall on several preceding days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a rock slide on Highway 152 near Emory Pass on the Sierra/Grant County Line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over Colorado provided adequate wind shear and instability to produce a severe weather outbreak across central and eastern sections of New Mexico.  Several of the storms became tornadic while others produced large hail, damaging winds and flooding rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail pounded the Belen area. Dozens of vehicles were damaged and numerous building had windows broken. Several street lights were damaged and trees were stripped of leaves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough, moving in weak northwest flow aloft, tapped rich surface moisture, causing scattered thunderstorms to develop.  Large hail was reported in Torrance and Lincoln Counties with flash flooding in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along Bonito Canyon and other small drainages southeast of Corona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough, moving in weak northwest flow aloft, tapped rich surface moisture, causing scattered thunderstorms to develop.  Large hail was reported in Torrance and Lincoln Counties with flash flooding in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred near Ancho.  A fence near a home was taken down by the flowing water.  Rainfall was measured at 2.76 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Though the air mass was not quite as moist through a deep layer as earlier in the month, there was still sufficient moisture for heavy rainfall amounts. The addition of a dry layer in the mid levels brought a threat of damaging winds and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of up to 2 inches fell on the South Tularosa Fire burn scar above Mescalero, resulting in excessive runoff. Logs washed on to Route 10, and much debris blocked residences. A fish hatchery was destroyed which killed all the fish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Though the air mass was not quite as moist through a deep layer as earlier in the month, there was still sufficient moisture for heavy rainfall amounts. The addition of a dry layer in the mid levels brought a threat of damaging winds and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded throughout central Luna County according to the Luna County Road Department.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered power and phone lines were down throughout Deming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful early autumn storm system over the Great Basin spread strong winds and deep moisture across much of north and central New Mexico beginning early on October 11th. Strong to severe thunderstorms first developed over the southwest portion of the state and spread quickly north and east into the central valley and eastern plains by mid afternoon. High winds also developed by the afternoon over the far western portions of the state.

Heavy rainfall occured across Southeast New Mexico and the Permian Basin as an early season cold front moved south across the area. Precipitable water values of up to 1.7 inches aided in the development of isolated areas of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hobbs city police department reported flash flooding from a thunderstorm in and near Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level short wave trough pushed eastward across the state Monday afternoon. A cold front draped across the central high plains provided focus for the development of a few severe thunderstorms late in the afternoon.

An isolated severe thunderstorm developed over extreme northern portions of Lea County ahead of a southward moving upper level disturbance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail occurred for a 15 minute duration.
